mnemonics: Make Incredibly Lazy Students Understand Lists And Tuples Successfully (MILSUST)
Many Intelligent Learners Successfully Understand Lists And Tuples (MISULT)
keyPoints: Lists are ordered collections of items, similar to arrays in other languages.
To append an element to a list, use the '+=' operator or the 'append()' method.
To insert an element at a specific position in a list, use the 'insert()' method.
To remove an element from a list, use the 'remove()' or 'pop()' method.
Indexing in Python starts at 0, so the first element is at index 0.
Slicing in Python allows you to get a subset of elements from a list.
Tuples are ordered, immutable collections of items, similar to arrays in other languages.
To create a tuple, use the '()' operator or the 'tuple()' function.
Indexing in tuples starts at 0, just like in lists.
Slicing in tuples works similarly to slicing in lists.
To access an element in a tuple, use the 'tup[ index]' syntax.
To modify an element in a tuple, use the 'tup[ index] = new_value' syntax.
Tuples are useful when you need to store a collection of items that shouldn't be changed.
